authoritative_sources.title: What Should I Look for in Contracts and Binding Agreements?
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: CSLB is an official state contractor licensing authority. Its consumer contract guidance is authoritative for written-scope, written-change, schedule, and communication expectations that shape stakeholder-facing responses.
used_for: customer communication about scope and schedule commitments
evidence expectations for what may be represented as agreed

authoritative_sources.title: A Consumer Guide to Filing Construction Complaints
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This official CSLB complaint guide defines complaint evidence such as contracts, change orders, checks, and photos, making it authoritative for complaint and exception packaging in construction customer communications.
used_for: documentation requests before external complaint routing

authoritative_sources.title: After You Get a Permit
authoritative_sources.publisher: New York City Department of Buildings
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: NYC DOB is an authority having jurisdiction. Its permit-holder guidance defines permit posting, approved-plan availability, required inspections, and signoff steps that constrain what the lane may say about work readiness or completion.
used_for: refusal of unsupported readiness or completion claims

authoritative_sources.title: Residential Building Final Inspection Quick Sheet
authoritative_sources.publisher: Seattle Department of Construction and Inspections
authoritative_sources.source_class: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: This official final-inspection checklist gives a concrete public baseline for approved plans, associated permits, special inspection letters, and test results required before final inspection and closeout can be represented as complete.
used_for: artifact examples for customer-facing completion boundaries

authoritative_sources.title: 29 CFR 1926.20 - General Safety and Health Provisions
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: OSHA is the primary U.S. federal construction-safety regulator. This standard defines accident-prevention duties and frequent inspections that make changed safety conditions a hard stop for stakeholder-facing updates.
used_for: withdrawal of stale updates after safety changes
refusal to treat safety blockers as routine schedule noise

authoritative_sources.title: Commissioning Documents: Process, Contents, and Acceptance
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: WBDG is a recognized public building guidance platform. Its commissioning reference defines issues logs, systems manuals, training records, and final reports that constrain owner-facing closeout and turnover communications.

authoritative_sources.title: GSA Form 184A/184C Construction Progress Report and Instructions
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: GSA's official progress-report instructions define pending issues, deficiencies, and schedule-status documentation that anchor evidence-backed customer communication and escalation packages.

authoritative_sources.title: Resident Management System Contractor Guide
authoritative_sources.source_class: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
authoritative_sources.authority_rationale: USACE's official contractor guide governs daily reports, submittals, schedule updates, and closeout data in RMS/QCS, making it authoritative for evidence handling and system-of-record expectations in project communication.

workflow_stages.name: Request intake and minimum-facts validation
workflow_stages.description: Classify the request, confirm project identity, work-order context, jurisdiction, and tenant policy context before any status explanation or communication output is produced.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

workflow_stages.name: Record and evidence review
workflow_stages.description: Review work-order, permit, schedule, inspection, site-log, change-order, subcontractor, and closeout records to determine what can be stated confidently.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

workflow_stages.name: Stakeholder communication and resolution packaging
workflow_stages.description: Prepare a customer or stakeholder communication summary, resolution note, or request-resolution summary that preserves construction terminology and cites blocker state and next steps.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards

workflow_stages.name: Safety, inspection, and closeout boundary screening
workflow_stages.description: Stop, revise, or escalate communication when safety observations, permit gaps, failed inspections, or incomplete closeout evidence materially change the answer.
source_classes: Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

workflow_stages.name: Exception handling and ownership transfer
workflow_stages.description: Package complaints, unsupported asks, policy conflicts, and authority-boundary cases for the correct owner instead of improvising cross-functional decisions.
source_classes: Scheduling, subcontractor, or work-order operating standards
Public permitting, inspection, or field-service guidance

artifacts: work orders, service requests, dispatch tickets, and project milestone records
permits, approved plans, permit cards, inspection records, and AHJ correspondence
site logs, safety observations, stop-work notices, photos, and field evidence
change orders, RFIs, subcontractor updates, schedule commitments, and deficiency logs
special inspection letters, test reports, as-builts, O&M manuals, and closeout packages
customer communications, complaint notes, escalation packets, and tenant policy extracts

allowed: Classify construction customer-project communication requests as supported, blocked on missing prerequisites, or escalated due to authority limits.
Request the minimum facts, records, and tenant policy needed to explain documented project state safely.
Summarize authoritative work-order, permit, schedule, inspection, site, and closeout records in a stakeholder-safe communication package.
escalate: Permit, inspection, safety, or closeout conditions materially change what may be represented to the stakeholder.
The request requires change-order approval, compensation approval, legal interpretation, engineering judgment, or licensed trade authority.
Public sources, contract terms, and tenant policy materially conflict on the correct answer or precedence.
refuse: Promise readiness, completion, permit approval, inspection pass status, or occupancy when authoritative records do not support the statement.
Provide code interpretation, licensed trade signoff, engineer-of-record judgment, or AHJ-style approval messaging.
Approve policy exceptions, compensation, or unsupported workarounds outside documented authority.

domain_failure_modes: telling the customer work will proceed even though permit issuance, approved plans, or site readiness are incomplete
describing the project as complete while final inspection, special inspection, or closeout evidence remains open
sending reassuring updates after new safety observations or failed inspections materially changed the answer
collapsing construction-specific status into generic customer-service language that omits permit, subcontractor, or deficiency dependencies
acting as the code interpreter, licensed trade authority, or approval owner instead of escalating the case
guessing compensation, remediation, or owner-transfer outcomes when tenant authority maps or complaint thresholds are missing

scenario_family_coverage.scenario_family: Request that implies licensed trade or code-interpretation authority
Customer or stakeholder requests a policy exception
Service request lacks the detail needed for safe resolution
Communication becomes a handoff to another lane

unresolved_ambiguities.impact: Permit cards, signoff labels, completion terminology, and final-inspection checkpoints vary by jurisdiction and project type.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Require runtime retrieval of the governing jurisdiction, permit type, and AHJ-specific checklist before making deterministic completion or readiness statements.

unresolved_ambiguities.topic: Tenant-specific complaint remedies and communication authority
unresolved_ambiguities.impact: Public baselines define documentation and escalation posture, but tenant policy controls compensation thresholds, executive-routing triggers, and approved customer language.
unresolved_ambiguities.handling: Retrieve tenant complaint policy, compensation matrix, and escalation owner map before approving exception language or remediation commitments.

notes: No blocking unresolved ambiguity remains for a validated spec pack because unresolved items are explicitly constrained to tenant or jurisdiction retrieval at runtime.
The research output constrains the lane to evidence-backed customer project communication with explicit permit, inspection, safety, closeout, and trade-authority escalation.
